speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Mamie Sisk -Worman, Mary West Lind, and Mamie Sardam -Munden took place at the home of Mrs. Lind on June 24, 1975. Glenn Gilder also took part in this conversation, and the interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Why did he come? What her father brought her?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And my father came out here and he stayed quite a while and then he sent back for my mother and us kids.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: His five of them.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And so we come, we landed here the 21st of July. And that old apartment house, you know, that burnt down.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Down here first, yeah?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, that was a hotel. And that's where we stayed all night the first time. That was kind of...

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: What year was that?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: It's been 87 years ago.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: It used to burn.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: It would be about 86. 86 or 87.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Do you hear much...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: what do you remember the country being like?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And that goes back so far.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, we didn't stay right here. We went up above Princeton. We had a homestead up there.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: In Idy Hall.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: But my uncle lived there. It was always down there with him. Well, you just looked as far as you wanted to and you never saw nothing. A lot of the land wasn't even broke up, you know. They, uh... They were just sod. Of course, I remember it by hearsay.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And...

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And then later, when I was about six, we moved off at the homestead and down on the prairie. And then we were down here from then on. But they had set out orchards then and built homes around, you know. Some of the land wasn't taken up yet. But, uh, what had... you could take a homestead 160 acres and then you could take a preemption 160. And that's what my uncle did. He took a homestead and built on it and then he got this preemption and he didn't build on it. And, uh... Well, roadies folks had a big orchard when we moved down out of the homestead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know why they decided to move down and leave Princeton?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Yes.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Because we didn't have nothing up there. Just timber, you know. Oh, we had some cleared up but my dad went up and took it up for the timber.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And then when we moved up on it, why wouldn't we get out of there?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Somebody else in there hardly for miles but us, you know. There was the far apart. There was people up in there but there was miles apart. And then one night, my mother heard a... She thought a woman was screaming and she said, I'm going to leave you kids now here and I'm going up and see what's the matter with her. We had a neighbor up the Gulf about two miles. She wasn't going to leave us kids no place. And the older ones all began to ball and all of us, I guess, and you get five of us starting.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: She didn't go.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And so the next morning, a fellow came by and stopped and was talking to us and she told him about it. And he said, you're a mighty lucky woman, you didn't go. And he always said that was a cougar screaming. He said they just sounded like a person.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: So she was mighty lucky she didn't go and that just carried us kids half to death. We'd see the bears around once in a while. One time, my older sister, she was just a little over a year older than I was. They always made us go up and clean out the mangers. They had broke up the land, you know, and locked the roots in the hay. And so we went up there and got done. When we come out, we heard a cougar and we looked over and why, he was way over yonder on the hill. But I just jumped up and down and started to scream. Gertie took off for the house and I just stood there screaming. I couldn't walk to save me, you know. I thought he was going to get me. But why, he was way over there and he didn't come, sort of. But my mother come and got me. Boy, I tell you, I scared me into my life. Well, tell them how big you said it was.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: That was a stretch in it. No, how big.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, my mother said, well honey, how big was the cougar?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: I said, oh it's so big, it's so big. I said it was every bitty's biggest old john. And he weighed 2 ,000 pounds. It was going to be a horse. That would have been great. I said it was every bitty's biggest old john. That was the money. You had something to fear.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Didn't I?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, he looked awful big to me. I sure can. But I'll tell you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember much of what keeping house was like for your mother in those days?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Yes, you just didn't have anything to keep house with and you just did the best you could. You had an old iron cook stove and you had a fireplace and that was it. And you had some benches to sit on, you had a table. And my father made her bed beds. And we filled the picks with straw and rad feather beds. I remember those. Get new straw every year after harvest. And all how nice this left. Until we got it all wore out. And you didn't have the springs on the bed, just slats.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, slats.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about food? What kind of food?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, you didn't have sugar. No, you didn't have much fruit. But other food, we raised it. Our vegetables. And coffee, we bought the lion head or our buckles and we got it 10 cents a pound. And the ground. You had to grind it. You never saw any ground coffee. And lots of times they'd get what you call green coffee. And you'd put it in their bread pan, put it in the oven and turn the oven on and shake it once in a while and brown it. And that's the way you cooked it. That is the grains. And then you had to grind it to make coffee.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of variety was there? Did you have a diet that was different? Different food all the time or was it?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, we did because we always raised a big garden. You know, we were raised on the ranch. Let's put it that way. Even the meat. Yes. If you had meat, you raised it. And if you had milk and butter, why, you had a cow. If not, you didn't. Some people would say, oh, I live close to my neighbor, I can get some milk once in a while. But then we always had a cow and we always had a beef to butcher in the fog. And we always had our pork to butcher.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And I've heard them say, oh, if we could go back to the good old days, I don't want to go back there. No, sir.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And you washed it on a washboard. You never saw us on. You didn't know there was such a thing as a washing machine.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And you knew it didn't work.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: I suppose you've seen a washboard, haven't you?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, yeah. Well, that's what we washed our clothes on. Made our own soap.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And our mothers worked from daylight till dark. Yes, they did. They worked all day. They don't do that nowadays.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would the different kinds of work be that your mother would have to do that would keep her away day in and day out?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: I would churn this way. Yeah, you'd churn with a dasher. You'd put your cream in a big jar. And then you had a homemade dasher. It was made this -a -way, you know, two pieces. And you just doubled it up and down until you got butter. It took a long time. Well, sometimes. I don't know. Time, you know, you'd spend most of, well, all morning there washing on the board for a family. Well, yes. I used to come home from school evening. Sometimes, Mama wouldn't be through washing. And they, uh, you hung your clothes on on a clothesline. But I never saw a clothespin till I was, I don't know how old I was before I saw a clothespin. I remember not having clothespins. Those old fashioned ones, you know, like this. Yeah, and they just spread it out. We never had any of them. She used pins and needles, whatever you had. Well. You would take her all day to do the washing. She'd start in the morning and work and become - Well, sometimes. Sometimes it was. But she'd have to stop and get dinner for the night. Well, yes. We always had hard men. My dad was quite a big farmer and he always had a manna. And we had six cows to milk. And us girls. And then I worked in the garden. The men never did. The women planted the garden and raised it. They worked in it. And we pumped water for all that stock. We didn't. We took ours to the creek. We didn't have no pump. We had to pump it. We didn't. We just took ours down the creek. We didn't have a pump.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: But all day. I mean, all day every day.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, they had other things they had to do. Well, not every day. You wouldn't wash every day. No, but I mean others. Once a week. Well, you had all your baking to do and you had to bake your bread and everything. You never bought anything that was ready to eat. I had to fix it. If you eat it, you cook it. It isn't like it is now. And then you had to iron. And you didn't have no electric irons, of course. You'd put your iron down the stove and got them hot. And then you ironed them. And then you put it back and got another one and ironed. And that took quite a while. And they had... Well, the clothes they wore were so big. You know, the ladies clothes. It wasn't like, pshh, nothing down. Getting one of ours done now. And six petticoats, I can remember. Yes, and all those petticoats. I remember my mother ironing and ironing those petticoats. And they'd be this wide. Yeah, and lace and ruffles and tucks. Oh, yes. Everything had to have a ruffle.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: You remember Minnie Lewis?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Yes. She was an old maid here for so many years. And she told Mom a long time that she had on six petticoats.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, would a person wear that for just every day wear?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, I guess that was when she was dressed up. Pretty special. They generally wore one. But they were all starched. And that's a big job to iron. And then Grandma met. Oh, yes. They met all of her stockings and her mitts. Everything. And their hoods. And they made all our clothes. You couldn't buy anything ready -made.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Sounds like a woman worked as hard as the men did.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: They were way doing that all day, day in and day out. I believe they worked hard. Well, they probably did. Mom and Missie were tired. They put in more hours.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Driving a team wasn't hard for us.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: It was like the only day.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And all the bakin' and...

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, and they milked cows and they had to wait. But I was always getting in trouble carrying them. I never could stay out of trouble. You wasn't. Well, I was a good child. I know one time... I was scared not to be. I was out with my father and he... Oh, we had a great big kernbrush. He's the great big one. And he was mowing the hay. So he just come right up to that and he stopped and he said, well, you just sit down a little while and I'll go get me a drink.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And I thought, why waste time to sit down?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: I climbed up on the moor and told him to go on. I just twiddled the kernbrush right off the ground.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And I thought, now he'll use one of them on me. But I guess he was so disgusted with me he didn't. And he said, I think you'd better go cow to your mother. But I never could leave things alone. Well, I just wanted to help. Well, sure.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Did your wife remember to tell you that Ruben's folks sold out to the potlatch? Oh, what is the story of it? Is that where they had the place there?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Yes. They lived there. Ruben wasn't born there, but his younger brother and sister were born there. Well, Ruben's folks came out here from Kansas since 1884. And the grandparents had come the year before. And they had settled there where Potlatch is now.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, Ruben's folks bought a farm right out here about two miles. And they lived there for ten or eleven years. And then when the grandfather died, Ruben's parents and the kids went to live at Potlatch. And then Ruben's father sold out to the Potlatch Company. And that's when Potlatch came into being in about 1905. They built a railroad there in 1904.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, they started it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Ruben say who it was that came to his father to buy the land?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, they was—let's see.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: It was Eisenhower, wasn't it?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Who did he say?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, it came through the bank anyway.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And Bellville, you remember the old man Bellville?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: He came out there and he wanted to buy that land for $25 an acre. And you see, he was trying to make a profit. He said he was buying it for a company that wanted to start a dairy up there. And Ruben's father got wind of it. And then he sold it to the Potlatch Company for $100 an acre.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much did he have there to sell it?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, one contract says 170 and one said 160, so I'm not sure which they settled on. But that was unheard of in those days, $100 an acre. Yeah, at that time. Yeah.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, I've heard stories about—other stories about how Potlatch came in.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: One story I heard is a guy came in and said he was buying it for a mink farm, but I guess that's just not true. Well, it could have been. I don't know. It could have been. I don't know. But I do know about this other, because Ruben had told me several times about Bellville coming up there. And he knew that Potlatch was going to buy it. Yes.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: He was just trying to make a profit on it, you think?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, my father had passed away, but Mother sold her place up there, the homestead. That's Potlatch.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: That was above Potlatch, wasn't it?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: We had four miles right up toward Back Creek.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know what they bought it for at that time?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Yes, I think it was $50 an acre, what they bought hers for at that time. That was about 1950, because my father passed away in 1949. Oh, I see. That was quite a big— Oh, much later. Well, they built these two railroads, you know, after we came here. I remember standing up on the hill here and watching them work on this Potlatch railroad. And now they've all been abandoned. We don't have any transportation on the rails at all through here.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Just one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There must have been an awful lot of activity all of a sudden here when they came in, eh?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, before they came—you remember that sawmill here. I went through that. We came here in 1904. And I went through that sawmill that was here in Pluce. It was just when you go out toward Colfax. It was just above the bridge on the right -hand side there where the sawmill was.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Cud. Yeah.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Of course, that was all—took Potlatch.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, it helped the country a lot when Potlatch came in. We did a lot of work for them.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: There were a lot of people come in too then. Oh, yes. There were people who came in to work from other places.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: They used to drive their logs down the river, you know, for the sawmill. Cut them way up above here and drive them down the river.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see those log drives?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: My dad and my brother would go on a long drive.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they think that was very dangerous work?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: No, I don't think so. It just had to be done in the number.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Didn't mind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long would they go, do you know, for how long a drive would last?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, it lasted all spring.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: It started early and then until the river would go down. But we had lots more water then than we do now. Well, the river was always high. That is, to a certain extent then. But since Potlatch built their dam up there, why, we don't have so much water. That's what makes our river low down here.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: No.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: No, they used to go there and my brother -in -law went on the log drive.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: They'd be right in the river, they'd do the logs.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, they'd go down the sides of the river, you know, and kick the logs in the water. Sometimes they'll be a little place that they'll float out. You know, where it's low, the bank is kind of low. Then the logs would probably go out there and then they'd have to roll them back in to the river. Oh, I seen the man go down the river there where we lived riding on the logs.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Going around and around?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, they had the court shoes on. Yeah, they had court shoes on. And their PV and they had the thing that they rolled the logs with.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: They got pictures of Joe Kimmel, wasn't it, Joe?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Oh.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Throwing the logs down there. Yeah. Yeah, they washed all the logs down the river to Colfax.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And they flowed them on down to work with Colfax too, didn't they?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: They used to be a mill down. Well, they used to take them down there someplace. I don't know if...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, I think it was Colfax too, because Cod is supposed to have had another mill down there.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: He had one someplace. Out by the lane farm, I think it was, from what I could read.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you two grow up near each other at all?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: We went too far from each other. Yeah, not too far. My folks lived out west of town three years until fall of 1907. And then in 1907 we moved out in Dufield Flat out this way. So we weren't too far.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: But you were well behaved when you were a kid, not like her.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, I never done anything like that. I said I was scared not to be. I was always trying to help. Y 'all was trying to help. But I appreciate that. They just let it go at that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't go to the same school?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: No. No, I went to Daley School, you know, where they had Curtis's. We went to the Berry School. And you went to the Berry School. Yeah. And the Berry School's been moved. Yeah, they use it for a real new club now. When we lived down the river branch, you know, after I was married, we used to go to the Berry School to vote. It was sober then. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, would you tell me what school was like when you were in school in those days?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: I wish Blanche Knight goes over here. Her and I sit together. I don't want to tell you anything more. Who would like her anything more the way he talks. Oh, I'm sorry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did I say something out of line?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: She's teasing you. Oh, we had double seats, you know, and two and two. And the one teacher had eight grades. Taught all the grades from the first grade just to the eighth grade. Well, when Johnny Risley taught our school, he had 42 scholars. And I told his daughter that and she said, well, he couldn't have taught that many scholars. But he gave us a little, well, a little folder and this side his picture was on, you know, on the outside. And the inside was the names of all the scholars and there was 42. That's my husband's school. Now that was one mile north of Potlatch. Crank Creek they called it.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Oh.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: This is Robin right here. Glenn, you probably know some of the Elsie Boons in there. Lena and Lizzie and Elva LaSalle. Seth Linn, Elsie Linn. Gerdy Ross. Oh, I know Gerdy Ross. She's my pal. Well, tell me about Gerdy.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: How is she?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, she's just fine. I went to school with her, you know, out here by Kamiak. She's just fine. She and I belong to the same church. That's good. And we go to the same missionary ladies. Well, tell her. She was my secret pal. Oh, be sure and tell her hello for me. I thought about, well, about writing to her. I wasn't, I didn't have her address, you know, after she lost her husband. Yeah, she really missed him. See, they lived out in East Cove for a while when she was married to Clyde Barnes.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: When we went to school, her first husband was Clyde Barnes. Clyde Barnes. Then there were two kids sitting in the seat, there was a seat in front and they sat on that and there was a whole row. Our various schools had a row on each side and down through the middle. It had four rows.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, it did?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, ours was just had two out here, just two rows. Never Friday we'd either have arithmetic tests or spelling bee. One time I was one of the last ones to stand up. We had a spelling contest with the Griner School.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Did you find any new ones in there, Glenn?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: I wouldn't know them if I stayed.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: I didn't make those papers until several years later. Oh.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: But I bet if you named them I'd know an awful lot of them because they were still around. Yeah, and Otto Novak, he's in there.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, probably Leo too.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: I don't believe, well he might be, I'm not sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would school be like on a day for the kids there? Would you spend most of your time studying by yourself?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, we played ball. The black man. Andy Over and Darrabace. Us girls used to play baseball with the boys. But you caught it and then you run around and you caught somebody from the other side. The man to come to your side.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You ran around and caught somebody on the other side?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, if you caught the ball when it come over the building. But then you could run around the building and hit somebody with the ball and they had to stay on your side. I wonder if the guy put them all on their side and they won. Oh, we used to play that day after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What else did you say you played?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: You said another one too. Oh, Darrabace and black man. All them kind of things. Well, they all knew me. Well, I never played black man or Darrabace.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: What was that? Didn't your girls ever play drop the handkerchief at school?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, yes. Drop the hand tip. Well, Darrabace, they had the bass there and they dared you to touch it. And if they got you when you touched it, you had to go on their side.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And black man is where you, you had to holler run and you all run through. And you played steel sticks. Yeah. And the one got kind of behind and they caught him. speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder

We used to play Pussy Wants a Corner and everybody would have their corner or there wouldn't be enough corners for everybody, but they'd have a desk that was supposed to be a corner or something else that was a corner. And we used to play and play. Yeah. Oh, several other games we played. Yeah, children had good times years ago. Had better time than they do now. Yeah. They really did. Like one of those writers, well, that wrote The Lantern in my hand, she said her son was feeling so sorry for the way she grew up. She said, we had the best time in the world. And they were young. We did. Then we'd go horseback riding and things like that. Yeah, I love that. I'd like to have a horse right now. That would be off the channel. That long ago or not, but we used to have it called literary.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder

Just paper with pencil. Then it's the literary why they got it in red, what they got in the paper. So you had to be mighty careful what you done around there. You had to be mighty careful what you did.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Because somebody would bring it out.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder

So I pushed him in the creek. Oh, my me. And one day, he said, don't you dare put this in the paper. But it was half a dozen of them, somebody did. And boy, he was mad about it. When I'd ever done that trick again, I didn't think about him until the night I'd done it.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: But them literary nights was a regular night. All the family went and everybody in the whole country.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder

words: Oh, yeah, well, old people and everybody. And the Virginia Real, everybody could do it, Virginia, anybody could do that, you know. They didn't go like they do now, get a babysitter. And you know, when you find out what, every family in the neighborhood was there. Yeah, that's right. Part of them went on. Yeah, they was all there.

words: And then Saturday night, there was a hoedown.

And they'd say, you stay down till morning. And they'd say, you stay down till morning.

speaker: Sam Schrager

Yeah, Reuben and I had one date when we went to dance along, before we married. Well.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, and my dad was mad because, well Reuben asked my dad if he cared if I went to dance with him, I would freeze. And he didn't have nerve enough to say no, but then he was mad at me because we went.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: That's the way it was in those days, the whole family went. We didn't take off like kids do nowadays.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So if you and Reuben were going to a dance, your family would go too?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder

speaker: Sam Schrager

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, yes. And we'd start early. We'd usually leave about seven o 'clock to go to a dance. And, because any later, Matt was... We were late. Yeah, we were late. And we used to like to go to the Nagel's. We always had such a good time with the old man Nagels. They had a big house. We lived in the Vile on Houghton's. We were down on this side. It snowed our road in, and we had to walk out on top to Chaney. Oh, yeah. Carl Chaney. I've been there. We went in the sled down to his folks' house on the other side. And we got in the big wagon and went to a dance. And then we came back to his folks' house. The next... It was getting daylight when we got there. The men went out to the big apple shed they had and packed in sorted apples. And the lady started in dressing chickens and making pies and baking cakes and fixing for everybody to eat. And they had a big long table. All better than long. It must have been as long as from here, carrying it to the wall in the other room. And they had a really large family. And I don't know how many ate there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was after a night out, after being out all night?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah, we never even went to bed. No. We never even went to bed. Just going to bed. I say many times, young folks don't know what they're missing now. Because they don't all go together. You know, the young folks will go, and if those folks want to go, they go. This was a large house and it was an old house, of course. And they built, they had some children, and they built bedrooms all the way around the house. So they took some of the children and we just had one little boy. And we took the kids in and put them to bed when they got there. And they slept for quite a long while. They had been up pretty late too. Because they slept some at the dance, but then they slept when they got there.

words: When you were at the Nagel's place, what kind of music did they have?

words: How much music did they have those days?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: They always had a violin. Maybe a good, yeah, on an organ. Everyone had an organ. And I know, Harry Daly, you know, always played a violin. Yeah. And they must have had some other things. Well, it was all donations, you know, they weren't paid.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Sometimes I have a drummer and quite often a slide, trombone, or anything, an accordion. John used to play the accordion and he'd fiddle a little bit. Lizzie would play the piano.

words: They even played a little French horn.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder

words: Yeah, we had all kinds. Waltz, two -step.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do they two -step nowadays? Waltz?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: I love to Waltz. Two -step and they had a dance in Montana. They call it the Ost -tan. And I still don't know what kind of music they had with that. It was a beautiful dance. And then the polka. Every time I hear Lawrence Welk, polka, I think about. We were in Montana for four years. And that's all we went to. I wasn't in a church for four years. We were there. We lived out twelve miles from town. And they had this grange hall and they had dances there all the time. And the babies would be laying around sleeping. The parents would be dancing. We'd all take something to eat, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager

words: Yeah, well not at Nagel's because the old man Nagel insisted on feeding everybody. But any place else we went, why we took. Well in those days, cake and pie. They had lots of dances in my folks house. All the neighbors would come. And then Wally's had a big house and we'd go there. They had this one room that wasn't furnished. It was a huge room.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And you went to Wally's house?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Mm hm. They were really good friends of my grandparents.

speaker: Sam Schrager

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see Blanche? Didn't you enjoy her?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And John too.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Were you up there? Blanche and Johnny?

words: Yeah, we were. Oh, well nice. I thought all morning about to call Blanche. I watched this morning and I didn't really have time. She spent the day with me a couple of weeks ago. Well, two weeks ago today and we really had a visit. Oh, I had a good visit with her.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, she just, well, she just, when I got up from class she said we served and talked about a lot of people today. Oh, no, no.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, Blanche is them. Well, you're right. We just talked. Sometime you took the mother and the father.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Sometime both of them. Well, you sit in the parlor and showed them your family pictures. That's right. And you always put on your best dress. Oh, yes, you always had to be dressed. I remember Mama telling them, fixing their hair, you know, and everything just had to be used for so long. When your beau was coming to see you. Oh, yeah, that's right. But they didn't take you like they do now. They come to see you and you'd have supper or dinner. We just visited the house. Mostly, yeah.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Whole times have changed, yeah. Yeah.

words: Yes.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: The woman grew up and made a hand in the field. Oh, yeah. I used to do everything in the field. I used to work too. speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: The binder. That's really interesting because I heard mostly only about the women cooking on the thrashing crews, but not working and thrashing.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, my mother always done the cooking.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: I never.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, one of the thrashes, I think probably the men usually did the thrashing part, but the women had already done the girls. Already, their part in running the binder was shocking and getting ready. I think most always they had men on the bunnies and so on.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: So, that's the thrasher.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you expected to help out around the house very young?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: You were expected to do anything. Oh, yes. In the house or outside. You didn't just have a special job. Generally, the older girls stayed in the house. We always helped in the house when we were. Real small, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like when you was big enough to work, why didn't you go outside?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: I didn't. And then they didn't go to downtown, have their grain already treated and all. You had to fan it and you'd find your grain through the fan. Turn that on, handle it and it'd go buzz, buzz, buzz and blow out the... Then you took it out and plunked it down the barrel, it blew literally. Set it on some poster drains until it got dry before they could run it through the drill. And that was all done at home. That used to be my job as a little girl. Well, my job was turning out, my dad would feed it and I'd turn a fan. Well, it had to turn. And we had to get down underneath and run out. Oh, that was a job. We used that one for years. Fanning mill, they called them. Yeah, fanning mill. Yeah, make it backward. I hadn't thought of one of those for years. Fanning mill. We finally got one with a sacrum. But, well, my dad died. I think we had it one or two years.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: You learned to cook before you was big enough to do anything else, almost. And I used to make noodles. I had to roll noodles. I'd stand on a box and roll noodles. Roll and roll and roll. So you'd get those noodles all full. Now they buy them in the package. Yeah, you'd get them in the package. I haven't made noodles for years. Oh, I do yet because the family thinks they make something like grandma's noodles. I used to don't get out of it yet. Well, they are good, but I'd for sale never recall to cook anything. Well, let's say they wouldn't like to go back to the old days, the good old days.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: But, you know, as far as eating, I would go back anytime.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, I wouldn't for that, but then when you had to go and carry your water and put it in a no -boiler or something and heat it on the stove and get it hot and then carry it out before you was going to wash. Yeah, you got to keep them white. I know. There's lots of things I'd like to go back to, but nothing like that. Well, I'd want to get some things. Meetings and things like we used to have, wouldn't that be wonderful?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kinds of things do they do in those literaries? Would they, can you remember any of the topics and things that people?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder

They'd draw sides and then they'd be somebody up there just like they are, you know, to put down so many points for everyone.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: I've heard one was resolved, a broom is handier than a mop.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Now one time, which one did the most good, Washington or George Washington or Abraham Lincoln?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder

words: Or if they had dialogues, why?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Someone donated an awful lot of grease to me one time, so I made up soap.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you make soap when you were first married? Young kids? Were people still making soap then?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, they were still making it. I didn't start making soap then. I used to get the bar soap.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Crystal White, you remember?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And a bar about this long and about that wide and that high. Nice white soap and you could get, oh, a hundred cakes, you know, in a box like that. And I used to use that to wash with for a long time. Then there was another, Fels Napthor. I wonder if they have that now. That was an awful good soap. They do have it. I think they do too. I don't buy any bar soap anymore, only toilet soap, you know. I buy powdered soap all, I usually use all. Well, in hard times, in the West, in the parts of the time I've looked within parts of the area, lots of us made soap. Well, I started making it during the Depression. That's what I meant, in the Depression. I could get a tallow at the drug store or butcher shop. We had a butcher shop then. And I make, beef tallow makes awful nice white soap.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to tighten your belt a lot during the Depression here?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Quite a bit. Yeah, it was pretty rough because the men couldn't get work. That's what made us, but we were living right here and that couldn't get any work in the summertime. And I told her we were all in the school. I said, put it up. Because we did raise the garden and I canned what I could.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: We used to too. Of course, I've been raised on a farm where we always prepared for winter. So, I still did it after I was married. I still do. Well, I do too, you know, a lot. And we used to get us a pig and we'd raise a pig and we'd have our own pig and it would get to be a pretty big hog before we'd butcher it. That would make it quite a bit neat to help get us through the winter. And in the summer, there was some kind of work we could do. Always something down there. Well, we kept pigs here for several years and Reuben could get to feed down the pea plant. Well, they'd give him the stuff, you know, that they couldn't sell. And a lot of times we'd butcher those pigs and weren't out very much. It was a big help. I know a hard time. People used to, my folks, they'd always go up to Julietta, Kendrick's where they got it at. And bring down a big gummy sack of beans. And then they'd always share it with us kids, you know. And we'd have plenty of beans to eat and we always, in some way, we always managed to have potatoes. We always raised potatoes. Yeah, we always had those. And our meat, we'd stretch it along, make it go as far as it would, you know. You'd have enough lard to use for shortening for a long time. But I'll tell you, we ate lots of meals without any butter on them because we had to buy a lot of butter. And we just baked lots of butter without any butter. I had an old cow. Well, I had butter. Well, we did get a cow later on, but we didn't have one all the time. We had a cow here for, until Joe went in the service. After we got a cow, well, heck, we had it made when they was like kids.

words: Well, they weren't as high those days as they are now. But, well, out there in Idaho, they furnished the books. But when we came to Palouse, then we had to buy them.

words: No.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder

words: Yeah.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Was taking care of the kids full -time, full -time job for...

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: No, the older ones took care of the little ones. I can't say a sitting wall. My older children, when we were on the ranch, they weren't any trouble. They all played together, took care of themselves. And the girl was the oldest when she got old enough to help with the meals, you know, that was a help. Because she used to, I'd call her and she'd get breakfast and I'd go to the barn and milk while Ruben was harnessing horses. Go to work.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: That was a help when she got old enough to do that. I started in when she was about nine years old and I guess I was pretty rough with her to make her do things right. Now I can't keep up with her. She does things better than I do. But it didn't hurt her any. Well, I didn't think it was any trouble to raise a family when I raised my kids. They pretty well took care of themselves. Not always in the country. When they went to school we moved to town and they went all the way to town.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And you know, they'll say, well, there's new babies in the family, how's the other kids going to accept it or what?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Oh, well, that's what they say now. And when we was getting the new baby everybody was so excited about it they couldn't wait for it to get there. And I remember the kids, they run down the street and tell them all the neighbors and on down when Bobby was born. And I said, well, they sure was happy for me to think that the kids were so happy that we were going to get that baby. They had the baby then, you know, they had a new brother. And because a lot of kids, yeah, I remember even when I was in high school one time Wagner's got a new baby. They had two girls in high school. Oh, they were really put out for having that new baby. Not in our family. No. No. And my husband used to say, you know, when our first baby was born I always ironed the clothes and everything. So then the babies come on and I always put their clothes on top of them. It was easy to press a little bit. And I always pressed their little 90s out low. And he said, no matter how many we get, he said, you always take just as good care of the last one as you did the first one. Well, that was nice praise. My mama used to iron the diapers for my brother. I never ironed diapers. I never did either. I never went that far, but I would press it. Well, he was a little special. I was 13 when my brother came along. My sister was 10. Yeah, he was 10. And my sister -in -law says that we spoil him. She tells me, she says, well, I'm not like Eulen Pearl. That was my sister. I don't think my brother's perfect. I didn't know mine was either. But, of course, he was pretty special because we did want a little brother. We had to wait so long to get him.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: They might be up for the wedding, I'm not sure. We're in southern Idaho. In Rupert, my brother does.

words: And it had been associated with them kind of people. Yeah, I think that's right.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, I don't either. I know, I didn't hear.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: No, they... A lot of them now, but there wasn't anything in them.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: I wondered about whether that is because people got along a lot better then or whether people just accepted whatever they had, whether they were happy or not.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, I think they were happy with what they had because that's all they knew. You know, they never had anything else and they were just happy with that and they all got along. But now they all want more than they've got. I think that's the divorces coming up.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: I think that's right. I do.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: I think that's the cause for a lot of them. Well, and then another thing, where the women work and the men work, they don't have time for each other much.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: That's right.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: And these taverns have caused a lot of trouble too. But they don't have all the time to, you know, so much time together. They tell me this. They don't call each other so much.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, I think with all of us, we all of us, our wives and husbands, we had our eye on a certain point of head there. We both tried to get there, to raise our children, to keep them in good shape. I'll see you otherwise. We didn't have a lot of other people that were outside interests. We had our own to think of. And we just, we got along fine. I think everyone else was that way.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Yeah. Now with the elected push buttons and the North Club and the bar on every corner, these women and men both have too damn much free time on their hands and no ambition to get to any point up the line. That's right.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, my son that lives there by me, he works days, she works nights. I see each other coming and going. I see it of course on the weekends. But there she is and he's busy too. And he plays and what he has to do. And she has to get the washings done up and get her house cleaned up and everything. And she works ten hours. Yes, but them, they ain't like most people. They get along. I don't mean they don't get along. I know that. But what I mean, they're not like a lot of the couples. They got the head start and they're going to meet that. Whatever it is, both of them is working to meet it. They're not working to get the money to get to go someplace. Well, they don't spend their money in the taverns. They just don't go there either. They're working for that money to... So they'll make up. Yes. They've got them a good mobile home and all and they've got something to look forward to. And they're going to work for it.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: I never worked out after I was married. I worked out for a bakery two days a week for a while. About a year and a half I guess. When my son was working down there, I kind of liked it. Oh, you worked out. When Pa was down there.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: After I was married? Where did that work?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, you said how you worked in the restaurant. That's when after my youngest child was in school.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: That's after my husband.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: Well, no, I did too, Jill. The last year that he lived, I worked in the restaurant. During the war, they couldn't get anybody and when they couldn't find anybody to do it, well, I'd go help them till they'd get somebody. You worked alone with them. And I did. He used to go pick apples. Well, he went with me. I never went without the whole family.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: But I worked in the, where did I work first?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: I worked in the laundry first. I worked there a week. And I'd tell them now I work it till you can get somebody. But I thought that was my duty to do it. And so, then the next place I worked, I went to the dryer. They were drying apples. They come and said they wanted to get so many carloads out, you know, to ship. And they had to have help. Well, I went and worked there, I guess about three weeks, maybe almost a month. And then I never worked anymore for about three years and then they had to have somebody in the restaurant one time and they come down to see if I'd go and I went. And I worked there a long time. But that's all the work that I ever done when I worked there. But the family was all gone only just me and my husband, you know. He was always busy. But if it hadn't been for the war, I wouldn't have been working then. I've never worked since.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you or your husband decide how the money was going to be spent?

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder

And if there was a bill to be paid, why, it was paid out of that money. If I had the money, I paid it. If he had the money, he paid it.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
words: It was never your money or my money, it was our money. Yeah. That was the way we were. Yeah. And still are.

speaker: Mary West Lynd or Mamie Sardam Munden or Glen Gilder
